hourly_trends.label: Morning Ramp-Up
hourly_trends.trend_description: User started the day with email and Slack catchup. Focus is moderate and stress is low — a typical slow morning warm-up. No significant anomalies.

hourly_trends.label: Peak Morning Deep Work
hourly_trends.trend_description: Strong sustained focus session in VS Code. Focus climbed steadily from 78 to 88+ over 10 minutes with stress remaining low. This is the first peak cognitive window of the day — user was in flow state.

hourly_trends.label: Stressful Meeting
hourly_trends.trend_description: Stress spiked sharply during a Zoom meeting — rising from 41 to 64 in 6 minutes, the highest stress window of the day. Focus also declined. This triggered a triage check-in session at 09:36.

hourly_trends.label: Recovery + Focus Rebuild
hourly_trends.trend_description: After the triage session and medication reminder, user shifted to writing/planning in Notion. Stress dropped back to baseline and focus rebuilt steadily — good recovery arc.

hourly_trends.label: Inactive
hourly_trends.trend_description: No active device usage recorded during this hour. Likely a break, lunch prep, or away from device.

hourly_trends.label: Lunch Break + Brief Anomaly
hourly_trends.trend_description: Relaxed browsing during lunch. One unusual spike to focus_score 84.94 was detected at 12:07 while on Reddit — out of character for a break window. LLM flagged this as likely context switching or accidental trigger.

hourly_trends.label: Afternoon Deep Work
hourly_trends.trend_description: Solid second coding session. Focus ramps from 75 to 83 with stable stress. Performance is slightly below morning peak but consistent — user appears well recovered after lunch.

hourly_trends.label: Communication Strain
hourly_trends.trend_description: A short but stressful Slack exchange raised stress to 47 while focus dropped to 47. Pattern is consistent with a difficult message thread or urgent escalation. A triage notification was issued.

hourly_trends.label: Best Focus Block of Day
hourly_trends.trend_description: User hit peak focus for the day — 91.4 at 15:32. Stress was low and sustained. This is the strongest cognitive window across the full 24-hour period, likely benefiting from post-lunch recovery and minimal interruptions.

hourly_trends.label: Wind-Down Comms
hourly_trends.trend_description: End-of-day email checks. Moderate focus and stable stress — nothing remarkable. User is transitioning out of deep work mode.

hourly_trends.label: Late Afternoon Planning
hourly_trends.trend_description: Short Notion session — likely end-of-day planning or journaling. Focus is healthy and stress is declining toward evening baseline.

hourly_trends.label: Evening Unwind
hourly_trends.trend_description: Passive YouTube consumption. Focus and stress are at their daily lows — user is decompressing effectively. No alerting patterns.

hourly_trends.label: Light Reading
hourly_trends.trend_description: Kindle reading before bed. Gentle focus uptick with the lowest stress of the day. Positive end to the cognitive day — good wind-down behavior.

details.note: Morning dose. User acknowledged within 2 minutes.

details.trigger: Stress spike detected — stress_level rose from 28.4 to 63.9 over 6 minutes during Zoom session
details.note: User accepted the triage prompt and entered a 5-minute check-in.

chat_log.message: Hey — I noticed your stress jumped quite a bit during the meeting just now. How are you feeling right now, on a scale of 1 to 10?
Honestly, like a 7. The meeting got tense, my manager was questioning my estimates in front of everyone.
That sounds frustrating, especially being called out publicly. Did the situation feel resolved by the time the meeting ended, or is there still tension hanging over you?
Still hanging. I don't know if I need to send a follow-up or just let it go.
Got it. Is this a pattern with your manager, or did it feel like a one-off today?
Bit of a pattern lately, but today was worse than usual.
That context is helpful. Before we wrap up — do you want to take 5 minutes away from your screen, or would you rather get back to something you're in control of, like your own work?
I'll get back to work. Focusing usually helps me reset.
That tracks with your data — your best focus sessions follow stressful events. Go for it. I'll flag this pattern for your daily report.

outcome.resolution: User chose to resume work. Stress declined within 30 minutes.

details.note: User dismissed without confirming. Possible lunch distraction.

details.trigger: Focus drop and stress rise detected during Slack session — focus_level dropped from 75 to 47, stress rose from 27 to 47

chat_log.message: I caught a pretty sharp dip in focus and a stress rise while you were on Slack just now. What was going on?
Got a message from a client saying they're unhappy with the delivery timeline. I wasn't expecting it.
Unexpected bad news tends to hit hard. Did you reply already, or are you still figuring out how to respond?
Haven't replied yet. I closed the thread because I couldn't think straight.
Good call — replying when stressed usually doesn't go well. Is this something you need to handle today, or can it wait until tomorrow morning when you're fresh?
Probably can wait until tomorrow. They're in a different timezone.
Perfect. I'd suggest scheduling 15 minutes tomorrow morning for it. Want me to add a reminder? And in the meantime — are you okay to keep going today?
Yeah, add the reminder. I'm going to go back to coding, that'll help.
Reminder added for 09:00 tomorrow. Your afternoon code sessions tend to be your strongest — you're in a good window for it.

outcome.resolution: User deferred client reply to next morning. Returned to VS Code — focus recovered to 88+ within 40 minutes.

reminder_created.note: Reply to client re: delivery timeline

details.note: Routine notification dismissed. User was in a deep work session — non-critical.

details.trigger: Evening check-in — end of active day review
details.note: User dismissed. No intervention needed — metrics were calm during evening unwind.

details.trigger: Pre-emptive alert — stress accelerating during Zoom, 4 minutes before peak
details.note: User snoozed the alert mid-meeting. Check-in was re-triggered at 09:36 after meeting ended.
